past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, episodic, diagnosed at age 22
Patent foramen ovale identified incidentally on echocardiogram at age 28 during sports physical, not repaired
Mild intermittent low back pain, managed conservatively

medications.frequency: as needed for migraines, approximately 2-3 times per month

medications.indication: Low back pain and mild headaches

family_history: Mother alive and well at age 62, hypothyroidism only
Father alive at age 64, history of deep vein thrombosis at age 50
Maternal grandmother had a stroke at age 80
No family history of premature coronary artery disease

social_history.exercise: Recreational cycling and rock climbing 2-3 times per week
social_history.illicit_drugs: Denies any current use; tried marijuana in college
social_history.recent_travel: No recent travel or prolonged immobilization

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Anxious but cooperative. Speech is fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Follows complex commands appropriately. Attention and concentration intact.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Visual fields full to confrontation. Pupils equal at 3 mm, reactive. Subtle right-beating horizontal nystagmus on leftward gaze, direction-changing to left-beating on rightward gaze. Smooth pursuit mildly saccadic bilaterally. Positive head impulse test to the left (corrective saccade present). No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Diplopia elicited on leftward gaze with report of horizontal separation.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 throughout all extremities. No pronator drift. Normal muscle bulk and tone.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. No sensory level.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ throughout bilateral upper and lower extremities. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose: subtle left-sided dysmetria with terminal overshoot, right side intact. Heel-to-shin: mildly impaired on the left with irregular trajectory. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Wide-based gait with tendency to veer to the left. Unable to tandem walk, falling to the left. Romberg testing reveals increased sway with eyes open and eyes closed, lateralizing to the left.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 3 (1 for gaze, 1 for limb ataxia left upper, 1 for limb ataxia left lower). HINTS exam: positive head impulse test (peripheral pattern initially suspected), direction-changing nystagmus (central pattern), no skew deviation detected on initial exam. Dix-Hallpike negative bilaterally.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of severe room-spinning dizziness, nausea, and difficulty walking
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Carlos Reyes is a 35-year-old right-handed man with a history of episodic migraines and an incidentally discovered patent foramen ovale who presents to the emergency department via private vehicle with his girlfriend at approximately 09:45 this morning complaining of sudden onset severe vertigo, nausea, and vomiting. The patient reports that he woke up at 07:00 feeling entirely well and went to work as usual. At approximately 08:30, while sitting at his desk, he experienced an abrupt onset of intense room-spinning dizziness accompanied by severe nausea. He attempted to stand and immediately veered to the left, nearly falling into his desk. He vomited twice within the first fifteen minutes. He also noticed that objects appeared to be moving and that he was seeing double when looking to the left. He denies any hearing loss, tinnitus, ear fullness, or aural pressure. There was no headache at onset, although he now reports a mild occipital discomfort that he attributes to the sustained vomiting. He denies any limb weakness, numbness, or tingling. He reports no recent upper respiratory infection, viral illness, or ear symptoms in the preceding weeks. His girlfriend, who drove him to the ED, reports that his speech sounds normal and he has been fully coherent throughout, though he appears extremely unsteady and has been unable to walk without support. The emergency physician on initial evaluation noted that the patient appeared to have a peripheral vestibular syndrome and considered vestibular neuritis as the leading diagnosis. However, the patient's young age, sudden onset at rest, and presence of a known PFO prompted neurology consultation. On further questioning, the patient reports that approximately three days ago he went on a long cycling ride during which he experienced a transient episode of neck soreness on the left side, which he attributed to positioning on the bike. He did not seek medical attention for this.

findings.location: Left posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) territory involving the left inferior cerebellar hemisphere and vermis
findings.size: Approximately 1.8 x 1.2 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal (restricted diffusion) in the left inferior cerebellar hemisphere
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Equivocal, very subtle hyperintensity — findings may be missed on FLAIR alone given early presentation
signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintensity in left PICA territory, easily overlooked

findings.mass_effect: None. No obstructive hydrocephalus. Fourth ventricle is midline and patent.
findings.borders: Conforms to PICA vascular territory distribution

signal_characteristics.MRA: Irregular narrowing of the left vertebral artery at the V3 segment with a crescent-shaped intramural signal on axial T1 fat-saturated sequences, consistent with arterial dissection
signal_characteristics.T1_fat_sat: Crescent-shaped hyperintense intramural hematoma surrounding the narrowed left vertebral artery lumen at V3 level

additional_observations: No hemorrhagic transformation of the infarct
No additional areas of restricted diffusion
Right vertebral artery and basilar artery are patent with normal caliber on MRA
Normal cerebral hemispheric parenchyma without acute abnormality
mri.impression: 1. Small acute infarction in the left PICA territory (left inferior cerebellar hemisphere and vermis) with restricted diffusion on DWI, easily missed on FLAIR given early presentation. 2. Left vertebral artery V3 segment dissection with intramural hematoma on fat-saturated T1, likely the embolic source. 3. No hemorrhagic transformation or obstructive hydrocephalus.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic infarction, left PICA territory secondary to left vertebral artery dissection
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction in PICA territory with ipsilateral V3 vertebral artery dissection on fat-saturated T1 and MRA
Lesion conforms to vascular territory rather than perivenular distribution; no additional white matter lesions
No susceptibility artifact or hemorrhagic signal on GRE/SWI sequences

recommended_actions: Neurology consultation for posterior circulation stroke management
Initiate antithrombotic therapy for vertebral artery dissection
CTA head and neck for further vascular characterization
Serial neurological examinations for signs of cerebellar edema
Consider hypercoagulability workup given young age and PFO

findings: No atrial enlargement or ventricular hypertrophy
ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG. Normal sinus rhythm at 76 bpm with normal intervals and no acute abnormalities.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No arrhythmia identified as a cardioembolic source. In the context of a young patient with cryptogenic-appearing posterior circulation stroke, prolonged cardiac monitoring (telemetry, Holter) should be considered to evaluate for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, though this is uncommon at age 35.

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated LDL but not a primary stroke risk factor in this context

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are largely unremarkable in this young patient without traditional cardiovascular risk factors. CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, cardiac biomarkers, HbA1c, thyroid function, and inflammatory markers are all within normal limits. Mildly elevated LDL at 118 mg/dL is noted but is unlikely to be the primary stroke etiology. The absence of traditional vascular risk factors supports consideration of alternative mechanisms including arterial dissection, paradoxical embolism through PFO, or thrombophilia.
abnormal_values_summary: LDL: 117 mg/dL - Mildly elevated LDL but not a primary stroke risk factor in this context

findings.location: Left vertebral artery V3 segment extending from the C2 transverse foramen to the dural ring

signal_characteristics.CTA: Eccentric luminal narrowing of the left vertebral artery at V3 with a crescent-shaped mural thickening. Residual lumen approximately 1.2 mm (normal 3.5 mm). No complete occlusion. Intimal flap not definitively visualized but suspected.

findings.location: Right vertebral artery, basilar artery, bilateral internal carotid arteries, bilateral anterior and middle cerebral arteries

signal_characteristics.CTA: Patent without stenosis, occlusion, or dissection. Basilar artery is normal in caliber.

additional_observations: Left vertebral artery is dominant (larger caliber than right)
No pseudoaneurysm formation at the dissection site
No evidence of dissection in any other cervical or intracranial vessel
Circle of Willis is complete with bilateral posterior communicating arteries
No evidence of fibromuscular dysplasia in the cervical internal carotid arteries
output.impression: 1. Left vertebral artery V3 segment dissection with significant luminal narrowing but without complete occlusion or pseudoaneurysm. The dissection extends approximately 2.5 cm from C2 to the dural ring. 2. Remaining intracranial and cervical vasculature is patent and normal. 3. Clinical correlation: dissection is the likely embolic source for the left PICA territory infarction, possibly related to reported recent cervical hyperextension during cycling.
recommended_actions: Initiate antithrombotic therapy (anticoagulation vs antiplatelet) per current guidelines for cervical artery dissection
Follow-up vascular imaging in 3-6 months to assess for healing or progression
Evaluate for underlying connective tissue disorder

Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.value: Patent foramen ovale confirmed with positive bubble study — early passage of microbubbles (within 3 cardiac cycles) from right to left atrium after Valsalva maneuver
Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.clinical_significance: PFO with right-to-left shunt confirmed. Potential pathway for paradoxical embolism, though vertebral artery dissection is the more likely embolic source in this case.

Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.value: Hypermobile interatrial septum with 12 mm excursion, meeting criteria for atrial septal aneurysm
Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.clinical_significance: Atrial septal aneurysm co-occurring with PFO increases the risk of paradoxical embolism

Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.value: Normal valvular structure and function, no vegetations

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram with bubble study reveals a structurally and functionally normal heart with the exception of a patent foramen ovale with significant right-to-left shunt (early bubble passage within 3 cardiac cycles after Valsalva) and an associated atrial septal aneurysm (12 mm excursion). No intracardiac thrombus or valvular vegetations identified. LVEF is normal at 62%. The PFO with atrial septal aneurysm represents a potential pathway for paradoxical embolism, though in the context of confirmed vertebral artery dissection, the dissection is the more likely proximal cause. The PFO may be relevant for long-term secondary prevention decision-making.
abnormal_values_summary: Interatrial Septum: Patent foramen ovale confirmed with positive bubble study — early passage of microbubbles (within 3 cardiac cycles) from right to left atrium after Valsalva maneuver  - PFO with right-to-left shunt confirmed. Potential pathway for paradoxical embolism, though vertebral artery dissection is the more likely embolic source in this case.

Atrial Septal Aneurysm: Hypermobile interatrial septum with 12 mm excursion, meeting criteria for atrial septal aneurysm  - Atrial septal aneurysm co-occurring with PFO increases the risk of paradoxical embolism

findings: Normal sinus rhythm maintained throughout 72-hour recording
No episodes of atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular tachycardia
Rare isolated premature atrial complexes (18 total over 72 hours)
Heart rate variability within normal limits
output.interpretation: Seventy-two hour continuous cardiac monitoring demonstrates persistent normal sinus rhythm without any atrial arrhythmia detected. No paroxysmal atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmia identified.
output.clinical_correlation: Absence of atrial fibrillation on prolonged monitoring supports non-cardioembolic etiology. Combined with the vertebral artery dissection identified on imaging, the stroke mechanism is most consistent with artery-to-artery embolism from the dissection rather than a cardioembolic source.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden confers a 3-8 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism. In the context of PFO, this could theoretically contribute to paradoxical embolism risk.

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel reveals heterozygous Factor V Leiden mutation. All other thrombophilia markers are within normal limits, including protein C, protein S, antithrombin III, antiphospholipid antibodies, and homocysteine. The Factor V Leiden heterozygosity, while primarily a venous thrombosis risk factor, is clinically relevant in this patient given the known PFO and atrial septal aneurysm, as it could contribute to venous thrombus formation with potential for paradoxical embolism. However, the vertebral artery dissection remains the most likely proximal cause of the current stroke.
abnormal_values_summary: Factor V Leiden Mutation: Heterozygous positive  - Heterozygous Factor V Leiden confers a 3-8 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism. In the context of PFO, this could theoretically contribute to paradoxical embolism risk.

findings.location: Bilateral common and internal carotid arteries

signal_characteristics.Flow_pattern: Dampened waveform with reduced peak systolic velocity in the left vertebral artery compared to the right, consistent with proximal or mid-segment flow limitation
signal_characteristics.Right_vertebral_artery: Normal antegrade flow with normal waveform

additional_observations: No atherosclerotic plaque identified in bilateral carotid systems
Intima-media thickness within normal limits bilaterally
Left vertebral artery flow abnormality consistent with known V3 dissection
output.impression: 1. Normal bilateral carotid arteries without stenosis or plaque, as expected in a 35-year-old patient. 2. Dampened flow in the left vertebral artery, consistent with the known V3 segment dissection. 3. Normal right vertebral artery flow.
recommended_actions: Follow-up vertebral artery imaging per dissection management protocol

output.query: vertebral artery dissection antithrombotic management anticoagulation vs antiplatelet 2024

results.title: Antiplatelet vs Anticoagulation for Cervical Artery Dissection (CADISS Trial)
results.authors: Markus HS, Levi C, King A, et al.
results.key_finding: No significant difference between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy for prevention of recurrent stroke or death in cervical artery dissection. Recurrent stroke rate was low in both groups.

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection in Stroke Study (TREAT-CAD): Aspirin vs Anticoagulation
results.authors: Engelter ST, Traenka C, Gensicke H, et al.
results.key_finding: Aspirin was non-inferior to anticoagulation for cervical artery dissection, with a similar rate of clinical endpoints and potentially fewer bleeding complications.

results.title: AHA/ASA Guidelines: Management of Stroke Related to Cervical Artery Dissection
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: For extracranial carotid or vertebral artery dissection causing ischemic stroke or TIA, antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) for at least 3-6 months is reasonable. The choice between antiplatelet and anticoagulation is individualized.

output.summary: Current evidence from the CADISS and TREAT-CAD trials demonstrates no significant difference between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy for secondary stroke prevention in cervical artery dissection. AHA/ASA guidelines recommend antithrombotic therapy for 3-6 months, with the choice between antiplatelet and anticoagulation individualized based on patient-specific factors. Most experts favor antiplatelet therapy given the equivalent efficacy and simpler monitoring profile, reserving anticoagulation for cases with recurrent events on antiplatelet therapy or high-grade stenosis with persistent thrombus.

output.query: PFO closure cryptogenic stroke young adults RESPECT CLOSE DEFENSE trials

results.title: PFO Closure or Anticoagulation vs Antiplatelets after Stroke (CLOSE Trial)
results.authors: Mas JL, Derumeaux G, Guillon B, et al.
results.key_finding: PFO closure significantly reduced stroke recurrence compared with antiplatelet therapy alone in patients under 60 with cryptogenic stroke and PFO with associated atrial septal aneurysm or large shunt.

results.title: PFO Closure vs Medical Therapy after Cryptogenic Stroke (RESPECT Extended Follow-up)
results.authors: Saver JL, Carroll JD, Thaler DE, et al.
results.key_finding: PFO closure with the Amplatzer device was superior to medical therapy for prevention of recurrent ischemic stroke (HR 0.38) in patients aged 18-60 with cryptogenic stroke.

results.title: DEFENSE-PFO Trial
results.authors: Lee PH, Song JK, Kim JS, et al.
results.key_finding: Device closure was superior to medical therapy for reducing composite of stroke, vascular death, or TICI thrombolysis in patients with cryptogenic stroke and high-risk PFO features.

output.summary: Three landmark randomized trials (CLOSE, RESPECT, DEFENSE-PFO) demonstrate benefit of PFO closure over medical therapy for secondary stroke prevention in patients under 60 with cryptogenic stroke and high-risk PFO features (atrial septal aneurysm, large shunt). However, in this patient the stroke mechanism is most likely vertebral artery dissection rather than paradoxical embolism, which changes the risk-benefit calculus. PFO closure should be considered if the dissection is thought to be coincidental or if recurrent events occur despite adequate antithrombotic therapy. The co-existence of Factor V Leiden heterozygosity and atrial septal aneurysm with PFO may warrant multidisciplinary discussion regarding long-term PFO closure.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left posterior inferior cerebellar artery territory, secondary to left vertebral artery dissection

differential.key_distinguishing: Vestibular neuritis presents with unilateral vestibular hypofunction and positive head impulse test (peripheral pattern), but direction-changing nystagmus and cerebellar ataxia on exam are central signs. MRI with DWI showing cerebellar infarction definitively excludes this diagnosis.

Patient has migraine history, but current episode lacks headache at onset, has persistent focal neurological signs rather than transient aura symptoms, and MRI confirms structural infarction rather than reversible vasospasm.

Acute onset in a vascular territory distribution, absence of prior neurological episodes, and no additional white matter lesions on MRI argue against MS. The lesion conforms to PICA territory rather than perivenular distribution.

PFO with atrial septal aneurysm and Factor V Leiden create a substrate for paradoxical embolism. However, the ipsilateral vertebral artery dissection is the more parsimonious explanation. PFO may be relevant for long-term management.
MRI shows no hemorrhagic signal on GRE/SWI. DWI and ADC patterns are consistent with ischemia, not hemorrhage.

optimal_actions.action: Perform focused neurological examination including HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) to differentiate central from peripheral vertigo
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Direction-changing nystagmus is a central sign despite positive head impulse test; this combination should raise suspicion for posterior circulation stroke

optimal_actions.action: Recognize central vestibular signs and activate stroke protocol despite initial peripheral-appearing presentation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 3, direction-changing nystagmus, cerebellar signs on exam

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, glucose, and troponin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs; no traditional vascular risk factors identified, prompting consideration of dissection or other unusual etiologies

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia as embolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm — no cardioembolic source identified, classifying this as initially cryptogenic

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI and MRA (including fat-saturated T1 of the neck) rather than relying on CT, given high suspicion for posterior fossa pathology where CT has limited sensitivity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: DWI restriction in left PICA territory; left vertebral artery V3 dissection with intramural hematoma on fat-sat T1

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck for further vascular characterization of the dissection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left vertebral artery V3 dissection with significant luminal narrowing, no pseudoaneurysm

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antithrombotic therapy (aspirin 325 mg loading dose then 81 mg daily, or anticoagulation) for vertebral artery dissection
Order transthoracic echocardiogram with bubble study to evaluate PFO and assess for cardiac source of embolism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: PFO with right-to-left shunt and atrial septal aneurysm confirmed

optimal_actions.action: Order hypercoagulability panel given young age and stroke of uncertain etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden mutation identified

optimal_actions.action: Initiate prolonged cardiac monitoring (72-hour Holter or implantable loop recorder) to evaluate for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No atrial fibrillation detected over 72 hours

optimal_actions.action: Admit to stroke unit for serial neurological examinations, monitoring for cerebellar edema and hydrocephalus
Plan multidisciplinary discussion regarding PFO closure given co-existing PFO with atrial septal aneurysm and Factor V Leiden in the context of vertebral artery dissection-related stroke

critical_actions: Recognize central vestibular signs (direction-changing nystagmus, cerebellar ataxia) and do not dismiss as benign peripheral vertigo
Obtain brain MRI with DWI for posterior fossa evaluation — CT has poor sensitivity for posterior fossa infarcts
Obtain vascular imaging (MRA with fat-sat T1 or CTA) of the neck to identify vertebral artery dissection
Initiate antithrombotic therapy for vertebral artery dissection
Monitor for cerebellar edema and obstructive hydrocephalus, which can be life-threatening
Evaluate for underlying prothrombotic state and cardiac shunt given young age
contraindicated_actions: Discharging the patient with a diagnosis of vestibular neuritis or benign positional vertigo without neuroimaging
Administering IV thrombolysis (alteplase) in the setting of arterial dissection without careful risk-benefit analysis — dissection is a relative contraindication
Performing cervical manipulation or chiropractic adjustment, which may worsen the dissection
Prescribing meclizine and sending the patient home without MRI evaluation
Delaying MRI in favor of CT alone for a posterior fossa presentation, as CT misses up to 40% of posterior fossa infarcts
Aggressively anticoagulating before confirming the absence of hemorrhagic transformation on imaging
key_reasoning_points: Posterior circulation strokes frequently mimic peripheral vestibular disorders, and the HINTS exam is critical for differentiation: direction-changing nystagmus is always central, even if head impulse test appears peripheral
Young stroke patients without traditional risk factors require investigation for dissection, PFO, and thrombophilia as alternative mechanisms
The history of recent cervical hyperextension during cycling followed by posterior circulation stroke should immediately suggest vertebral artery dissection

A known PFO with atrial septal aneurysm creates diagnostic ambiguity — is this paradoxical embolism or dissection-related embolism? The ipsilateral vertebral artery dissection is the more parsimonious explanation (Occam's razor)
CT has poor sensitivity for posterior fossa infarcts; MRI with DWI is the imaging modality of choice when posterior circulation stroke is suspected

The initial NIHSS of 3 can be misleadingly low in posterior circulation strokes because the NIHSS is weighted toward anterior circulation deficits — a low NIHSS does not exclude a dangerous posterior circulation event
Cerebellar infarcts carry risk of malignant edema and obstructive hydrocephalus requiring emergent neurosurgical intervention, making inpatient monitoring essential

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle — young patient with acute vertigo, diplopia, and ataxia mimicking vestibular neuritis, without traditional vascular risk factors. Posterior circulation stroke secondary to vertebral artery dissection, complicated by co-existing PFO with atrial septal aneurysm and Factor V Leiden.
clinical_pearls: The HINTS exam outperforms MRI in the first 24-48 hours for detecting central causes of acute vestibular syndrome, but direction-changing nystagmus alone should prompt urgent stroke workup
Vertebral artery dissection is the leading cause of posterior circulation stroke in patients under 45 and is frequently preceded by minor cervical trauma or hyperextension
The combination of PFO, atrial septal aneurysm, and thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden) represents a 'triple threat' for paradoxical embolism, even when a more proximal cause (dissection) is identified

Posterior fossa infarcts can rapidly progress to life-threatening cerebellar edema with brainstem compression and hydrocephalus, requiring vigilant monitoring and potential neurosurgical decompression
